Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'd try to set aside time, but between working part-time to make ends meet and dealing with constant anxiety about everything, I'd end up cramming whenever I could. I'm someone who learns by doing, so I'd often skip the reading and jump straight into practice problems or hands-on activities. Most of my studying happened in intense bursts, usually late at night when the fear of failing really hit me.
Q2: My pattern was almost entirely driven by deadlines and panic. I couldn't maintain regular study times because my work schedule kept changing and I never knew when I'd have energy left. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd spend three days straight trying to catch up. The anxiety would build until I couldn't ignore it anymore, and that's when I'd finally sit down and work.
Q3: Usually it was fear that got me to log in—fear of missing something important, fear of failing, fear of falling too far behind. Sometimes I'd get a notification about an upcoming deadline and my heart would race, and I'd immediately check what I needed to do. Other times I logged in just to calm myself down, to see if things were as bad as I imagined or if I still had time.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me—it was more like a source of stress I avoided until I couldn't anymore. I tried to make it a habit early on, but my attendance was already so low and I felt so behind that logging in just reminded me of everything I was struggling with. The only consistent thing was checking it frantically before deadlines.
Q5: The video content was actually really helpful when I could focus on it. I watched a lot of videos because I could pause, rewind, and actually work through things at my own pace. Discussion posts were useful too because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Anything hands-on or practical felt more valuable to me than just reading text.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With my limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on screen for hours made my anxiety worse. I also avoided discussion boards sometimes because I felt too behind to contribute anything meaningful. It felt embarrassing to ask basic questions when everyone else seemed to understand.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just consuming information passively. Anything that let me practice and actually do something—that's what kept me engaged. If it felt too theoretical or disconnected from the assessments, I'd lose focus quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're scared and stretched thin, you focus on survival—what's required, what's graded, what will hurt you if you miss it. Optional stuff always got pushed aside because I was barely keeping up with the mandatory work. There was guilt about skipping them, but not enough time or mental energy to act on it.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded videos and materials to study offline because my internet at home isn't always reliable. I'd take notes by hand while watching videos on my phone during bus rides to work. A lot of my learning happened with printed notes from classmates or textbooks I borrowed from the library. The LMS was just the starting point, not where the actual learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems on paper, rewatching downloaded lectures, or studying with a friend from class—none of which showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours making sense of concepts by physically working through them, drawing diagrams, talking it out. The LMS would just show that I logged in once to download something, not the five hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then study it repeatedly offline over the next week. My login might show one quick session, but I'd have that material open on my phone for days. It was more practical given my situation—I couldn't always count on being somewhere with good internet when I needed to study.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety peaked and I felt completely overwhelmed. There was a period mid-semester when I was working extra shifts, barely sleeping, and the fear of failure was paralyzing. I'd open the LMS, see how much I'd missed, and just shut down. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped somewhat, but getting started again felt impossible when I was that scared.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Fatigue from work made it hard to concentrate. Confusion about course material made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how lost I was. When I had competing responsibilities—work, family stuff, basic survival—the LMS was the first thing I'd sacrifice, even knowing it would hurt me later.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or whether I'd make it through the semester. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was actually sinking in. My body was there, clicking through pages, but mentally I was checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I didn't understand where to start. The bigger the task, the more I'd avoid it because the anxiety about doing it wrong was paralyzing. I knew it was making things worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope with the fear. It's a terrible cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could cut through my avoidance and force me into action. I'd ignore something for weeks, then work frantically in the last 48 hours. The LMS would show me as inactive for long stretches, then suddenly very active right before due dates. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I survived.
Q17: Feedback was complicated. Good feedback motivated me and made me feel like maybe I could do this. But negative feedback or low scores would send my anxiety through the roof and make me want to avoid the course entirely. Sometimes I'd delay checking my grades for days because I was too scared to see the results. The fear of disappointment was overwhelming.
Q18: The structure was honestly hard for me to follow with my attendance being so low. I'd miss classes and then feel lost about what was happening in the course. When I did engage, having clear deadlines helped, but the weekly structure assumed a consistency I couldn't maintain. I needed more flexibility and clearer catch-up pathways for someone in my situation.
Q19: The video content made things easier because I could learn at my own pace. But the sheer number of courses and materials—19 online courses—was overwhelming. Having so many discussion requirements was hard because I felt too behind to participate meaningfully. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus optional would have helped me focus my limited energy.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, checking things, starting to watch videos but not finishing, downloading materials to study later. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just panicking, clicking around, trying to figure out what you've missed. The fear keeps you logging in even when you can't actually produce work.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS activity was low, but I still managed a Merit because I was doing a lot of work offline and putting everything into the assignments that counted. I'd study intensively with downloaded materials and handwritten notes, then submit decent work. The system couldn't see my actual effort because it happened outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when people are active, what they click on, whether they meet deadlines. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the emotional context, the quality of attention versus just having something open. They can't tell if someone is genuinely learning or just anxiously clicking through materials. They miss the human struggle behind the data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when your mind is present and you're genuinely trying to learn, even when it's hard. For me, real engagement often happened in moments of clarity between waves of anxiety—when I could focus enough to actually absorb something and feel like I was growing.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I was all semester, how much anxiety shaped every decision I made about the course. It can't show the hours I spent studying on buses, in break rooms at work, or late at night with downloaded materials. It can't capture the mindfulness exercises I did to calm down enough to study, or how hard I fought just to submit work despite feeling like I was drowning.
